Jewish Tradition

Gersonides (Ralbag)

1288 – 1344

An Aristotelian Jewish philosopher on intellect, immortality, and the limits of divine knowledge.

I treated your question as a reconstruction problem, not just a summary problem. Looking across Wars of the Lord I–IV in Hebrew, the later cosmological books in English translation, and the biblical commentaries together with English, French, and Italian scholarship, the clearest result is that Gersonides does not have an isolated “human psychology.” He has a graded ontology of mindedness: embodied human faculties, the acquired intellect, the Agent/Active Intellect, the separate intelligences or angelic minds, and God as First Intellect. Human mindedness only makes full sense inside that hierarchy. Francophone scholarship is especially helpful here, because it insists that in Gersonides the commentaries and the Wars are mutually illuminating, and that theology is not an optional appendix to his science but part of what justifies the whole enterprise.

My main thesis is this: Gersonides’ philosophy of mind is an Aristotelian, strongly relational, and deeply hierarchical account in which the human soul is an embodied complex of powers; actual intellection requires the Agent Intellect; immortality belongs not to the whole psycho-biographical self but to the intelligible order one has actually acquired; and the minds of God and the separate intelligences are not distractions from the philosophy of mind but the very conditions that make his account of cognition, prophecy, providence, and freedom coherent.

I will use “material intellect” for the hylomorphic intellect, “Agent Intellect” and “Active Intellect” interchangeably, and “acquired intellect” for sekhel ha-niqneh. Those are the standard pivots of the discussion in Book I of the Wars, which Gersonides presents as his systematic treatment of the survival of the soul and the nature of human intellect. Robinson is surely right that questions of intellect, cognition, conjunction, and immortality are among his most sustained concerns, and that Book I is where he most programmatically tries to defend a doctrine of individual immortality.

1. The basic architecture of the human soul

Gersonides begins from a decisive asymmetry. He says the intellect is the part of the soul most eligible for survival and eternity, because the other soul-powers carry out their operations through bodily instruments and perish with the body. That already rules out any simple picture in which “the soul” is a single immaterial ego that just happens to use a body. For him, the human soul is stratified: most of its powers are embodied, transient, and organ-dependent; only intellection even opens the question of postmortem persistence.

At the same time, his psychology is not exhausted by the contrast between body and intellect. In Book IV he explicitly includes practical intellect and various affective and desiderative structures among the means by which human life is preserved and guided. Human mindedness therefore includes practical cognition, purposive desire, and ordinary animal-style orientation to good and bad, even though theoretical intellection remains the highest and only immortalizable activity.

The material or hylomorphic intellect is not, for Gersonides, an already-actual separate substance hidden inside us. Against views he associates with Themistius and similar positions, he insists that the human potential intellect is a mere preparedness or capacity. Robinson’s reconstruction, based on Book I, goes further and glosses it as a disposition generated in the imagination. Whether one states this maximally or cautiously, the point is the same: the human intellectual principle begins as embodied potentiality, not as a ready-made self-subsisting thinker.

That is why imagination matters so much. Gersonides argues that phantasms alone cannot explain how the human intellect reaches intelligible natures; some actualizing factor must abstract or make explicit the intelligible content latent in imaginative representations. So his view is neither crude empiricism nor pure illuminationism. Human thought begins from sense and imagination, but it becomes actual thought only through an external noetic cause.

2. The Agent Intellect and the externality of thought

The Agent/Active Intellect is therefore central, not ornamental. Gersonides treats it as a separate incorporeal intelligence that is both a cause of existence in the sublunar world and a cause of thought, precisely because it contains the full intelligible order of that world: all forms and their relations, the rational pattern or law of sublunar reality. Human thinking works because there is an objective intelligible order already in act outside the human thinker.

This is one of the most important consequences for a reconstructed philosophy of mind. In modern terms, Gersonides is radically noetic-externalist. Human thought is not self-grounding. Its actuality depends on an external intelligence whose content the human knower can only partially mirror. That is why remarks about the Agent Intellect are not “theological distractions.” They do the explanatory work that a later philosopher might assign to innate structures, pure rationality, or a fully self-sufficient subject.

Gersonides is also careful to distinguish the Agent Intellect from God. In the later books he argues that the Agent Intellect is not one of the heavenly movers, and he seems to hold that it emanates from the movers of the spheres while surpassing any individual mover in its comprehensive grasp of sublunar order. It knows the lower world perfectly as derived from the heavens, but it does not know the whole celestial-terrestrial plan with the completeness proper to the First Intellect. That larger totality belongs to God alone.

3. Acquired intellect: what survives, and in what sense?

Gersonides’ most characteristic move is his doctrine of the acquired intellect. In Book I he defines it as the perfection the Agent Intellect brings about in the material intellect. He then radicalizes the Aristotelian identity thesis: in intellectual cognition, knower, knowing, and known are one, and the acquired intellect is nothing over and above the intelligibles actually acquired. The highest state of human mind is therefore not a bare subject “having” thoughts, but the achieved organization of intelligible content itself.

This is also why the acquired intellect is immortal. Gersonides’ basic idea is that what the human intellect acquires is not a bodily trace but participation in intelligibles that are already actually present in the Agent Intellect. Since these intelligibles are not corruptible in the way bodily states are, the acquired intellect can survive. His Proverbs commentary restates the point very directly: intellectual apprehension is not something one simply loses like an ordinary sensory episode, because the acquired intellect is eternal.

But he does not think the human mind fully grasps the Agent Intellect itself. Book I explicitly says that a human being cannot apprehend the Agent Intellect completely; at most, one attains a partial “union” by acquiring intelligibles that are also in the Agent Intellect. That is why later scholarship, especially Feldman’s, is right to distinguish between a merely epistemic union and any ontological fusion or numerical identity between the human knower and the Agent Intellect.

Postmortem survival is thus austere but real. Gersonides says that after death the soul apprehends together the intelligibles it acquired in life, with greater joy because bodily impediments are gone; yet it cannot acquire any new intelligibles after death, since the senses, imagination, and related powers required for acquisition are no longer available. Degrees of felicity differ according to the amount, quality, and ordering of what one has acquired.

The hardest question is whether this is “personal immortality” in a strong sense. Robinson is surely right that Gersonides is trying to defend individual immortality. But an important Francophone line of interpretation presses the opposite point: the acquired intellect may be less a surviving consciousness than a body of eternal conceptual knowledge with only a thin unity and a fragile individuality. The texts really do support both pressures. Gersonides individuates disembodied intellects by the quantity, quality, and synthesis of what they know, and he speaks of postmortem joy; yet what survives is plainly not the full embodied person with sensation, appetite, imagination, and biography intact.

So the surviving subject in Gersonides is best described as a noetic self, not a full psycho-physical self. Inference from the texts suggests that what persists is not autobiographical memory in a modern sense but stable possession of intelligible order. That is a very different model of immortality from later religious or modern personalist views, but it is coherent within his system.

4. Content of mind: particulars, not just universals

One of the most philosophically interesting features of Gersonides is how anti-Platonic his view of mental content is. He argues that intelligibles do exist actually outside the human soul, in the Agent Intellect. But he also rejects the simple idea that human intellect knows pure universals as universals floating free of particulars. In Book I he argues that the intellect does not grasp the individual as this numerically singular thing, yet neither does it grasp a universal as universal in abstraction from individuals; rather, it knows an individual under an intelligible aspect, “some kind of individual.” Secondary scholarship captures this well when it says that the Agent Intellect contains the rational order obtaining in all individuals.

This matters because it shows that Gersonides is not trying to make the mind flee the world of particulars into a separate realm of abstractions. He is trying to explain how objective order is extracted from particulars and retained as intelligible structure. Robinson’s synthesis is helpful here: for Gersonides, all human knowledge comes through experience, induction, and learning; there is no innate stock of truths and no short path by supernatural illumination, not even for first principles. His philosophy of mind is therefore thoroughly world-involving.

5. Faculty psychology: imagination, dreams, prophecy

Book II shows how rich Gersonides’ psychology really is. He argues that true dream, divination, and prophecy all presuppose an efficient cause that knows the relevant ordered possibilities and produces the cognition in us. That cause is intellectual, ultimately the Agent Intellect. But the receiving faculty differs. Prophecy terminates in the perfected material intellect; dream and divination terminate in imagination. This one distinction already explains why prophecy requires wisdom, training, and intellectual perfection, whereas dreams and divinatory cognition can occur even in youths and fools.

Imagination is therefore not mere noise in the system. It is a genuine representational medium. When what is received is only imperfectly imitated or translated by imagination, cognition arrives as riddle, parable, or symbolic image; when the intellectual reception is stronger, the content is clearer and less figurative. Gersonides uses exactly this structure to explain why Moses’ prophecy could occur while awake: his receptive power was maximally perfected and the other faculties were unusually easy to subordinate. The soul, on this account, is one system whose powers can interfere with one another or be functionally isolated under special conditions.

This also connects cognition with agency. Gersonides says prophecy is teleologically directed to human felicity and moral redirection, whereas dreams and divination do not reliably have that function. He also treats the human intellect as the basis of choice and as naturally ordered toward the good. So rational mindedness in his system is not merely contemplative. It is the seat of the kind of cognition from which normatively guided action becomes possible.

Book IV broadens this into a larger psychology of guidance. Human beings are equipped with practical intellect for useful arts and with various tendencies that help them avoid harms and pursue goods. Higher providence works by a more refined version of the same logic: some persons are informed of future good and evil so that they can act differently. Cognition, desire, practical orientation, and providence are therefore continuous for Gersonides rather than separate philosophical compartments.

6. Divine mind

Gersonides’ philosophy of mind becomes fully intelligible only when we include the divine mind. God is not, for him, simply a bigger human thinker. The First Intellect’s activity is eternal self-intellection; unlike human thought, it does not fatigue, and unlike our knowledge, its knowing is causal rather than caused by things. In the language of the later books, divine knowledge is not universal or particular in the ordinary human sense, because God knows beings through the very order and existence He causes. God knows the law of existent things as a total whole.

At the same time, Gersonides is not simply a negative theologian in the Maimonidean mode. Francophone scholarship correctly emphasizes that he is more willing than Maimonides to allow a certain positive knowledge of God from divine actions and some positive predication that does not compromise divine unity. That matters for philosophy of mind because it means the human intellect’s ascent toward God is not empty apophatic silence; it is genuine, though limited, cognition of a higher mode of mind.

His treatment of future contingents is especially important. Gersonides argues that God knows future particulars insofar as they are ordered and delimited, but not which still-open alternative of a genuinely contingent human choice will be actualized while it remains open. For him this is not a defect in God. It is precisely knowing the contingent as contingent. In other words, divine cognition tracks the mode of being of its object. That theory of divine mind is what allows him to preserve human freedom.

7. Angelic minds and separate intelligences

Between God and human beings stand the separate intelligences or angelic minds. Each heavenly mover apprehends the order linked to its own sphere; the Agent Intellect gathers the sublunar order more comprehensively than any single mover; and God alone comprehends the whole celestial-terrestrial plan. Gersonides thus presents not one kind of mind but a scale of minds, ordered by scope, actuality, simplicity, and causal power.

This is exactly where theological and philosophical language intertwine. When distinguishing God from the Agent Intellect, Gersonides reaches for rabbinic angelological language to explain how the Agent Intellect can be extraordinarily exalted without being identical with God. So the “mind of angels” is not an alien add-on beside his psychology. It is the middle term that explains how human intellectual life can be related upward to divine order without collapsing either into God or into mere bodily mechanism.

8. The biblical commentaries are philosophically central

The biblical commentaries are indispensable for extracting a philosophy of mind from Gersonides. Older French scholarship already stressed that the Wars and the commentaries constantly refer to one another, and Italian scholarship on the Song of Songs underlines the same thing: his exegesis is fundamentally philosophical, integrating metaphysical, physical, and cosmological themes rather than merely decorating them.

The Song of Songs commentary is especially revealing. According to both English and Italian discussions, Gersonides reads the book as containing two subsidiary dialogues: one between the human material intellect and the Active Intellect, and another between the faculties of the soul and the material intellect. “Solomon” becomes the material intellect ascending toward actualization. Ultimate felicity is knowledge of God to the extent possible for humans, reached through knowledge of the created order and the sciences that lead upward from mathematics and physics through astronomy to metaphysics.

This multilingual reception helps correct a common flattening in Anglophone summaries. Gersonides’ scientific program is not independent of salvation. French scholarship puts the point sharply: theology justifies the scientific project. That is exactly why the human mind, the Agent Intellect, the separate intelligences, and God belong to one continuous inquiry.

9. A reconstructed Gersonidean philosophy of mind

Put systematically, the position looks like this.

First, mind is not primarily inward subjectivity for Gersonides. It is the actuality of intelligible form. That is why he can say that in intellection the knower, knowing, and known are one, and why the acquired intellect is nothing over and above what has been intellectually acquired.

Second, human mindedness is irreducibly embodied at the point of acquisition. The material intellect begins as potency; sense, imagination, and related faculties are required for learning; and after death no new knowledge can be acquired because those embodied instruments are gone. Gersonides is therefore not a Cartesian dualist avant la lettre. He is a hylomorphic thinker with a trans-bodily noetic residue.

Third, the human mind is relational all the way down. Actual thought depends on the Agent Intellect, which already contains the intelligible order of the sublunar world. In modern terms, this is a very strong kind of externalism: the mind’s best states depend on successful participation in an objective order outside itself.

Fourth, the content of mind is grounded in particulars. Gersonides rejects both a pure universalism and a purely sensory particularism. The intellect knows particulars under intelligible aspects, and immortality lies in acquiring the rational order that runs through individuals. His epistemology is therefore neither otherworldly Platonism nor nominalist collapse into singulars.

Fifth, personal survival is real but thin. What survives is not the whole embodied personality but the acquired intelligible order, individuated by what and how one has known. That gives him a doctrine of graded immortality and postmortem joy, but it leaves modern questions about memory, consciousness, and first-person identity only partially satisfied.

Sixth, divine and angelic minds are not exceptions to the theory but its upper registers. Human mind, Agent Intellect, separate intelligences, and God differ by degree of actuality, scope, simplicity, and causal efficacy. Gersonides therefore has not one psychology but a hierarchy of mindedness.

Seventh, prophecy and providence are cognitively continuous with ordinary psychology. The same broad architecture of faculties, receptive conditions, and external noetic causation explains ordinary learning, dream imagery, prophetic clarity, moral guidance, and differential providence. What changes is the receiving power, the degree of perfection, and the scope of the order received.

10. Final assessment

The deepest payoff of Gersonides’ philosophy of mind is that it offers a powerful alternative to consciousness-centered models. It is not built around private experience, self-presence, or introspective certainty. It is built around intellection, form, and the degree to which a knower becomes proportioned to objective intelligible order. On that picture, the highest human self is not given at birth; it is achieved.

The cost of the view is equally clear. If one wants a robustly personal afterlife with memory, character, imagination, and lived biography preserved as such, Gersonides gives only a strained approximation. But the gain is enormous: he can unify psychology, epistemology, cosmology, prophecy, providence, angelology, and theology into a single theory of mindedness. For that reason, remarks about the mind of God and the minds of angels are not just relevant to his philosophy of mind; they are structurally indispensable to it.

A natural next step would be a source-by-source map of the key passages in Wars of the Lord I–IV, Song of Songs, and Proverbs, organized by topic: intellect, imagination, immortality, prophecy, providence, divine knowledge, and separate intelligences.
